summary: Yegge uses the story of his Siamese fighting fish to draw a profound analogy about the limits of human intelligence. He describes how programming teaches us disciplined thinking but ultimately humbles us by revealing the boundaries of our reasoning ability. His betta fish methodically explored every inch of a vine in its tank, searching for an escape route with the thoroughness of a programmer doing a soundness proof. After five days of systematic investigation confirmed there was no way out, the fish died of apparent despair. Yegge argues that humans are intellectually little better off than that fish — we can easily envision problems millions of times more complex than what we can reason about. This is framed as part one of a larger series on how programmers see the universe.
key_points: Programming first inflates your confidence in problem-solving, then humbles you by revealing the hard limits of human reasoning ability
Systems can grow so complex that they must be abandoned entirely, and we can easily envision problems orders of magnitude beyond what any human or group can solve
The betta fish demonstrated remarkable intelligence by systematically exploring the physical nature of its tank — not just its topology — searching for an escape with methodical rigor
The fish's vine inspection was essentially a soundness proof of inescapability, conducted with the same systematic thoroughness a programmer would use
Humans occupy the same epistemic position as the fish: intelligent enough to perceive the boundaries of our understanding, but powerless to transcend them
money_quotes: In the end, programming shows us how small we are.
Eventually every programmer becomes entangled in a system that is overwhelming in its complexity.
He wasn't exploring the tank. He'd already investigated its topology for weeks. This time, he was exploring the nature of the tank.
Here was an intelligent prisoner, my captive, exploring the mechanics of his prison with a thoroughness that only the imprisoned can afford, looking for an escape with deathly tenacity.
I watched him use them all as methodically as any programmer to reason his way through to a soundness proof of the inescapability of his prison.
We like to think of ourselves as being pretty smart. Admit it. We do. But in the grand scheme of things we're intellectually little better off than that fish.
We can just as easily envision problems thousands or millions of times more complex: problems beyond the reasoning abilities of any person, any group of people, or even our entire species.

The scary thing is that it's very easy, as a programmer standing at the precipice of complexity, to envision systems that are orders of magnitude more complex, millions of times more complex, even unimaginably more complex.
key_insight: Programming teaches us just enough about systematic reasoning to recognize how vast and inescapable the limits of our own intelligence truly are — we are all fish in tanks, methodically proving we cannot get out.
